title: Multi-method Analysis of the Metal/Electrolyte Interface: Scanning Force Microscopy (SFM), Quartz Microbalance Measurements (QMB), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) and Grazing Incidence X-ray Diffractometry (GIXD) at a Polycrystalline Copper Electrode
abstract:
SURFACE AND INTERFACE ANALYSIS, VOL. 25, 548È560 (1997)
Multi-method Analysis of the Metal/Electrolyte
Interface: Scanning Force Microscopy (SFM),
Quartz Microbalance Measurements (QMB),
Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR)
and Grazing Incidence X-ray Di†ractometry
(GIXD) at a Polycrystalline Copper Electrode
W. Kautek,* M.
M. Sahre, P. Zhaos and S. Mirwald
Geu,
Laboratory for Thin Film Technology, Federal Institute for Materials Research and Testing, D-12200 Berlin, Germany
The successful application of various in situ and ex situ analytical techniques has been demonstrated at the buried
interface between a metal and an electrolyte. Scanning force microscopy (SFM), electrochemical quartz micro-
balance measurements (EQMB), grazing incidence x-ray di†ractometry (GIXD), Fourier transform infrared spec-
troscopy (FTIR) in the specular reÑection absorption mode and electrochemical charge measurements proved
complementary in the characterization of a polycrystalline copper electrode in alkaline 0.1 M sulphate, perchlorate,
Ñuoride and chloride electrolyte contact at pH 12. The surface exhibited a mixture of Cu(111) and Cu(200) grains
of the order of 100 nm. Repeated potential scanning in the double layer and passive oxide region, between Ô1.4 and
+ 0.1
resulted in a complete reorganization of the surface morphology but no detectable corrosion. The

accordance with a solid-state growth mechanism. The copper atoms were redeposited at crystallographically pre-
ferred sites, generating comparatively homogeneously oriented, narrow Cu(111)-edged grain ridges of length ¿200
nm. In the Cu(I) potential range, the formation of several monolayers of amorphous
could be conÐrmed.

KEYWORDS: in situ analysis; buried interfaces; quartz microbalance; scanning force microscopy; x-ray di†ractometry;
Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy; electrochemical corrosion; electrochemical conversion; copper
INTRODUCTION
Analysis of the metal/liquid interface has been per-
formed traditionally by electrochemical means, e.g.
currentÈpotential curves. This approach lacks direct
chemical and morphological information. An urgent
demand of in situ or in-process techniques therefore has
been deÐned in the last two decades. In order to provide
a maximum of unambiguous identiÐcation of interfacial
phases
and
processes,
a
multi-method
analytical
approach employing mechanical (mass, shape), spectro-
scopic (molecular vibration, x-ray di†raction) and elec-
trochemical (potential, charge) information has been
developed in this work.
* Correspondence to: W. Kautek, Laboratory for Thin Film Tech-
nology, Federal Institute for Materials Research and Testing, D-12200
Berlin, Germany.
s Present address: Department of Chemistry, University of Georgia,
Athens, GA 30602-2556, USA.
Corrosion studies relating to electronic equipment
are of particular interest due to immense, global invest-
ment in electronics, its high density and the small sizes
of the devices.1 Telephone switching equipment, for
example, has an expected life of decades, depending on
the interaction with the environment. Copper is one of
the most common metals used in electrical engineering
and electronics. Anodic dissolution of copper in alkaline
solutions has attracted a great deal of attention due to
its relevance to understanding the corrosion mecha-
nisms of copper.2 Electroformation and character-
ization of cuprous oxide Ðlms have become of interest
as an active material in various types of solar cells3h6
and as passive material in solar-selective surfaces.7h11
Oxide layers also seem to play a decisive role in the
electrode-less copper deposition involving formaldehyde
as reducing agent.12h16
Numerous electrochemical studies of copper contain
a number of conÑicting views, viz. the oxidation states,
composition and the mechanism of the anodically
formed passivating Ðlms.17h19 In oxygen-free alkaline
solutions, there is agreement on the formation of Cu2O

 METAL/ELECTROLYTE INTERFACE
549
and the Cu(II) species. In situ Raman spectroscopy20
and XPS studies on emersed electrodes21 showed the
presence of
in the Cu(II) potential regime. The
Cu(OH)2
breakdown of passive Ðlms is determined by the loss of
pH control at the surface due to proton generation.22
Copper, like any other metal in aqueous electrolyte
solution, is covered with a chemically bound oxygen
layer when a characteristic potential negative of the
equilibrium potential of oxide formation is exceeded
analogous to the underpotential deposition of metals on
foreign substrates.23,24 The structure of these thin layers
is not known. Especially at low coverages, they may be
pure chemisorption layers when the oxygen species is
bound to the unchanged copper surface. However, there
are indications that oxygen species also are present in
the entire double layer region down to potentials near
the hydrogen evolution.25h28 The electrochemisorption
of oxygen species in the potential region between hydro-
gen evolution and the Cu(I) oxide region of poly-
crystalline copper Ðlms in alkaline environment29 and
the electrodissolution potential in acidic environment,30
respectively, has been studied by surface-enhanced
Raman spectroscopy and electrochemical impedance
spectroscopy. In situ quartz microbalance measure-
ments (QMB) also used in this work revealed that
copper even dissolves in this potential region when per-
oxodisulphate is present.31
With increasing coverage at higher anodic potential a
site exchange between Cu and O (or OH) ions may
proceed at the metal surface, an oxide layer is formed or
dissolution
takes
place.
Several
authors
claim
a
dissolutionÈprecipitation mechanism (i) for both Cu2O
and CuO32h35 or dissolutionÈprecipitation of Cu2O36
followed by surface nucleation growth (ii) of CuO.37 A
change in the oxide growth mechanism of CuO from (ii)
to (i) has been discussed too.38 In the pH range 7.0È9.2,
however, direct growth (ii) was proposed for the entire
Cu(I)/Cu(II) system.39 Only at strongly alkaline condi-
tions ([OH~] [ 0.1 M) was dissolutionÈprecipitation
observed.40 An alternative model was suggested where
is the only direct growth product at lower poten-
Cu2O
tial and is an initial reaction product at higher poten-
tials, which is further oxidized to Cu(II).41,42 This Cu(II)
dissolves and leads to pores in
Finally, a
Cu2O.
overlayer is precipitated.
Cu(OH)2
This short review shows that there are still many con-
tradictory views on the partial reactions taking place at
the copper/electrolyte interface and shows how their
rate depends on the electrode potential, pH, thickness
and
structure.
Moreover,
substantial
morphology
changes
have
to
be
expected
during
and
after
oxidationÈreduction cycles, which are widely unknown.
Electrochemical investigations alone are not sufficient in
the elucidation of such surface conversion reactions. A
bundle of well-selected ex situ and in situ interfacial
analytical tools have to be employed. In this context, we
demonstrate the successful application of scanning force
microscopy (SFM) supported by other in situ and ex
situ techniques at a polycrystalline copper electrode:
electrochemical quartz microbalance (EQMB), grazing
incidence x-ray di†ractometry (GIXD) and Fourier
transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) in the specular
reÑection absorption mode.43
Scanning probe techniques like scanning tunnelling
microscopy (STM) and SFM have proved valuable in
the study of nucleation and growth of two-dimensional
and three-dimensional metal phases on metal electrodes
in
aqueous
electrolytes.44
Two-dimensional
under-
potential and three-dimensional deposits of copper on
foreign monocrystalline substrates like gold, silver45h50
and copper51 have been studied extensively, mainly by
in situ STM. Also, in situ SFM of copper at the micron
scale can contribute to the fundamental understanding
on the nanoscopic level. Thus, deposition essential for
electronic applications,52 corrosion of polycrystalline
copper in acid53 and the adsorption of oxygen species
at monocrystalline copper in acidic contact54 have been
reported. Actually, SFM data suggest stable oxide
monolayers in the thermodynamically forbidden poten-
tial region.
Quartz
microbalances
have
been
employed
to
monitor mass changes at copper surfaces in telecommu-
nication switching offices55 and natural environments.56
In contact with liquids (EQMB), they have been used to
monitor mass57h62 and viscosity63h65 changes at gold
electrode surfaces. The resonant frequency change *f of
the shear vibration of a quartz crystal is a†ected by
rigid mass changes *m. A complication is the surface
roughness. Trapping of liquid in surface cavities can
result in an additional mass component depending on
the amount of trapped liquid and the size of the cavi-
ties.66h68 Roughness change e†ects in the oxide mono-
layer region of gold, for example, could be ruled out by
ex
situ
STM
roughness
statistical
investigations.69
Open-circuit
spontaneous
dissolution
of
copper
in
oxygen-saturated acidic contact was studied by QMB.70
In the absence of oxygen, oscillations in frequency
response indicated a complex mechanism.71 Cyclic volt-
ammetric investigation of copper in alkaline media
resulted in irreversible mass increase after an oxidationÈ
reduction potential excursion into the region of Cu(II)
oxide formation.72 This was interpreted by the adher-
ence of remnant water molecules on the copper surface
that had previously been bonded in the oxide Ðlms.
Because these oxide Ðlms have been quantitatively
removed, this reasoning appears highly improbable and
therefore has been checked in the present study.
The combination of mass (EQMB) and morphologi-
cal (SFM) investigations cannot provide chemical infor-
mation.
This
can
be
achieved
by
in
situ
reÑectionÈabsorption FTIR spectroscopy at conducting
electrodes in electrolyte contact.73h82 The crystallinity
status of surfaces, Ðlms and coatings formed can be
addressed
by
x-ray
di†ractometry
(XRD).
Normal
instruments, however, do not provide sufficient sensi-
tivity when specimens are covered by electrolyte phases
and window materials. X-radiation of extremely high
intensity from synchrotronsÈextended x-ray adsorp-
tion Ðne structure (EXAFS) or x-ray adsorption near-
edge structure (XANES)È83 have been applied recently
to electrochemical in situ EXAFS investigations.84 Con-
ventional di†raction techniques with highly sensitive
solid-state detectors85 have been used for in situ experi-
ments (x-ray di†erential spectrometry),86 requiring long
data acquisition times (several days). The development
of commercial collimators and detectors, on the other
hand, opened up the possibility of using grazing inci-
dence x-ray di†ractometry (GIXD)87 for the in situ
characterization
of
the
solid/liquid
interface.

expensive and tedious synchrotron sources are not

Polycrystalline copper on optically polished 5 MHz
quartz oscillator crystals (KVG, Neckarbischofsheim,
Germany) served as electrode material (0.28 cm2 area
on both sides). The Ar`-sputtered Ðlms were treated in
for 30 s, extensively rinsed in bidistilled
water, blasted dry and transferred into the electrochemi-
cal cell under nitrogen. These electrodes were cycled in
the double layer region between [1.5 and [1.0 VMSE
until the frequencyÈpotential behaviour of the electro-
chemical quartz microbalance (see below) stabilized.
Electrolytes were prepared with analytical grade chemi-
cals and bidistilled water. PuriÐed (99.9999%) and
water-saturated nitrogen served for deoxygenation.
An electrochemical cell was integrated into the SFM
model Discoverer). The working
electrode was positioned on a steel base plate and was
contacted by means of a copper foil. A platinum wire
served as counter electrode and a saturated mercury
sulphate electrode (MSE) served as reference in the
EQMB measurements (see below). The electrolyte con-
tainment consisted of a Latex membrane. The top was
tightly closed with a quartz window. In the electrolyte
underneath, the cantilever was suspended and could be
optically addressed by the probe laser beam through the
top window. The scanner was a tripod type (24 lm).
Scan ranges were mostly 2 ] 2 lm2 with a resolution of
300 ] 300 pixels. Cantilevers used had a force constant
of 0.032 N m~1 with two v-shaped beams 200 lm long,
18 lm wide and 0.4 lm thick. The beam material,
including the tip (radius 50 nm), was
side was coated with 30 nm Au as a laser mirror.

Glancing incidence x-ray di†ractometry was run in a
BraggÈSoller x-ray di†ractometer system with a Ñat sec-
ondary monochromator and Ðxed Cu Ka tube (Seifert
XRD 3000TT) equipped with a specially designed elec-
trochemical cell. Working and counter electrodes were
embedded in an acrylic holder with electric contacts
outside the holder. Two, Ñat, gold counter electrodes
(2.5 ] 21 mm) were positioned parallel to the long side
of the working electrode (10 ] 21 mm). Two channels
enabled Ðlling and draining of the electrolyte. One tube
was connected to a glass vessel containing a saturated
calomel reference electrode (potentials given relative to
an elastic ring and silicon clue served as an x-ray
window. The electrolyte was Ðlled under potentiostatic
control of the working electrode. The thickness of the
electrolyte above the electrodes was kept to at least 1
mm during electrochemical cycling. The GIXD mea-
surements were run at Ðxed potentials with the electro-
lyte only a few micrometres thick to achieve sufficiently
low absorption of the x-rays by the electrolyte. The
grazing incidence angle ) was 2¡. A spectrum from
# \ 10¡ up to 30¡ took 10 min.

Scanning force microscopy (SFM) investigations
The above charge and mass data contain no informa-
tion about the inhomogeneity and microscopic morpho-
logical dynamics of the reactions considered. This is in
agreement with all electrochemical current and QMB
studies published to date, where conclusions commonly
assumed homogeneous surface reactions even on poly-
crystalline substrates. Ex situ and in situ SFM could
amend this deÐcit. Figure 4(a) shows the topography of
the original Cu sample. Fine grains with dimensions of
the order of 100 nm led to a moderately rough surface
(\60 nm) (Fig. 5). Repeated scanning between [1.4
as described in the previous section,
resulted in a complete reorganization of the surface
morphology: comparatively homogeneously oriented
narrow-edged ridges of length D200 nm have been
formed [Fig. 4(b)]. Rotation of the sample in the micro-
scope ruled out the possibility of depiction of cantilever
shapes. After emersion at the negative limit of the
ically Ñat regions, as can be seen in a line scan along the
axes of the ridges (Fig. 6). This is in agreement with in
situ SFM results on monocrystalline copper elec-
trodes.97 The dramatic morphology conversion proves

The successful application of various in situ and ex situ
analytical techniques has been demonstrated at a poly-
crystalline copper electrode in alkaline electrolytes at
pH 12. Scanning force microscopy (SFM), electrochemi-
cal quartz microbalance (EQMB), grazing incidence
x-ray di†ractometry (GIXD), Fourier transform infrared
spectrometry (FTIR) in the specular reÑection absorp-
tion mode and electrochemical charge measurements
proved complementary in the characterization of the
The original polycrystalline copper surface exhibited
a mixture of Cu(111) and Cu(200) grains of the order of
The EQMB measurements were able to resolve sub-
layer: the mass change correlated with less than one
Repeated potential scanning in the double layer and
passive oxide region, between [1.4 and ]0.1 VMSE ,
resulted in a complete reorganization of the surface
morphology but no detectable corrosion. The electro-
trolyte and the oxide Ðlm took place reversibly in
accordance with a solid-state growth mechanism. The
lateral migration of Cu ions in the oxide layers during
the oxide reduction occurred in distances of the order of
the oxide thicknesses attained, i.e. equivalent to D7
mono-layers or D80 nm. The copper atoms were
redeposited at crystallographically preferred sites, gen-
erating comparatively homogeneously oriented narrow
Cu(111)-edged grain ridges of length D200 nm.
